coredata.dc:title: Compressible, anisotropic lamellar cellulose-based carbon aerogels enhanced by carbon dots for superior energy storage and water deionization
coredata.dc:creator: Sun, Jiaming
Liu, Yushan
Wu, Zhenwei
Xu, Mingcong
E, Lei
Ma, Chuihui
Luo, Sha
Huang, Ju
Li, Wei
Liu, Shouxin
coredata.dc:description: Heteroatom-doped carbon materials have received great attention for applications in electrode materials. However, conventional heteroatom-doping methods sacrifice conductivity, stability, and specific surface area (SSA). Here, the carbon quantum dots (CDs) are used as carriers of N, P, O to form electron-rich regions promoting electron transport without decreasing stability and SSA. The CDs promote the formation of graphitic nitrogen in the composite, which effectively reduces their internal resistance by increasing the dielectric constant. Moreover, the orderly growth of ice crystals generates a unique bridged layer structure under bidirectional freeze-casting in a mixture of GO/CDs/microfibrillated cellulose, which gives the composite super-compressibility. Notably, the optimal sample has a 117% increase in specific capacitance. The CDs also improve wettability and thus reduce the charge transfer resistance giving a large desalination capacity of 32.59 mg g−1 (504 mg L−1 NaCl). This work illustrates the unique role of CDs in improving the electrochemical performance of composites.
coredata.dcterms:subject: compressible carbon aerogel
cellulose
carbon quantum dot
N,P,O co-doped
supercapacitor
deionization

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of ternary carbon aerogel of rGO/CDs/CMF

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Natural Science Foundation of Heilongjiang Province
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Natural Science Foundation of Heilongjiang Province

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The present work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 31890773 , 31971601 ) and the Natural Science Foundation of Heilongjiang Province ( YQ2020C016 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Fabrication and microstructures of rGO-CD180-CMF. (a) Schematic illustration of the fabrication of rGO-CD180-CMF by bidirectional freezing casting method. (b) TEM images of rGO-CD180-CMF. (c)–(e) SEM images of rGO-CD180-CMF. (f)–(g) Mapping of rGO-CD180-CMF.

(a) Compressive stress–strain curves at 80% strain (insets are digital photos at 80% strain); (b) Change in R/R0 with a repeatedly compressed for 20 cycles at 50% strain; (c)–(e) Strain-controlled switching of a LED connected in series with rGO-CD180-CMF.

(a) XPS spectra of as-prepared samples. High-resolution XPS spectra of (b) the fitted N 1s peaks of rGO-CD180-CMF and rGO-CMF (c) the fitted P 2p peaks of rGO-CD180-CMF and rGO-CMF. (d) Schematic diagram of functional groups of rGO-CD180-CMF. (e) XRD patterns of as-prepared samples. (f) Comparison of rGO-CD180-CMF and rGO-CMF nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherms and pore size distributions.

(a) CV curves at a scan rate of 20 mV s−1; (b) Galvanostatic charge-discharge (GCD) curves at a current density of 1 A g−1; (c) EIS spectra of rGO-CDx-CMF ; (d) CV curves at different scan rates; (e) GCD curves at different current densities; (f) Cycle test at 10 A g−1 of rGO-CD180-CMF.

Comparison of rGO-CD180-CMF and rGO-CMF electrochemical performances. CV curves at a scan rate of 50 mV s−1, GCD curves at current density of 1 A g−1, EIS spectra and specific capacitance in (a–d) 1 M NaCl, (e–h) 6 M KOH, and (i-l) 1 M H2SO4 aqueous solutions.

CDI performance of rGO-CD180-CMF: (a) Plots of conductivity vs. desalination time at different applied voltages measured in 33.0 mg L−1 NaCl; (b) Ragone plots in a 33.0 mg L−1 NaCl solution at different voltages and the inset is the plot of SAC vs. deionization time; (c) Ragone plots in different initial NaCl concentrations at applied voltage of 1.2 V and the inset is a plot of SAC vs. deionization time; (d) Comparison of desalination ability of rGO-CD180-CMF and rGO-CMF at an applied voltage of 1.2 V in 504 mg L−1 NaCl; (e) Schematic of the CDI testing apparatus.
The comparison of rGO-CMF and rGO-CD180-CMF about specific surface area, pore volume, and pore size distribution.

head.ce:title: Compressible, anisotropic lamellar cellulose-based carbon aerogels enhanced by carbon dots for superior energy storage and water deionization

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Bio-Based Material Science and Technology of the Ministry of Education, Engineering Research Center of Advanced Wooden Materials of the Ministry of Education, Northeast Forestry University, Harbin, 150040, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Bio-Based Material Science and Technology of the Ministry of Education
Engineering Research Center of Advanced Wooden Materials of the Ministry of Education

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Bio-based Material Science and Technology of the Ministry of Education, Engineering Research Center of Advanced Wooden Materials of the Ministry of Education, Northeast Forestry University, Harbin 150040, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Heteroatom-doped carbon materials have received great attention for applications in electrode materials. However, conventional heteroatom-doping methods sacrifice conductivity, stability, and specific surface area (SSA). Here, the carbon quantum dots (CDs) are used as carriers of N, P, O to form electron-rich regions promoting electron transport without decreasing stability and SSA. The CDs promote the formation of graphitic nitrogen in the composite, which effectively reduces their internal resistance by increasing the dielectric constant. Moreover, the orderly growth of ice crystals generates a unique bridged layer structure under bidirectional freeze-casting in a mixture of GO/CDs/microfibrillated cellulose, which gives the composite super-compressibility. Notably, the optimal sample has a 117% increase in specific capacitance. The CDs also improve wettability and thus reduce the charge transfer resistance giving a large desalination capacity of 32.59 mg g−1 (504 mg L−1 NaCl). This work illustrates the unique role of CDs in improving the electrochemical performance of composites.

ce:section.ce:para: The potential exhaustion of fossil fuels and uncontrolled use of naturally-available water resources have led to increasing global demands in developing sustainable energy supplies and desalination methods. Supercapacitor has been developed over the years into promising energy storage devices because the combinition of high energy density of batteries and high power density of traditional capacitors (Shao, El-Kady, Sun, Li, & Zhang, 2018). Meanwhile, capacitive deionization (CDI) is considered to be a promising water treatment technology because of durable, energy efficient, and cost-effective (Sun et al., 2020). Surprisingly, carbon materials with high conductivity, porous structure, chemical stability and easy modification have broad application prospects in both fields (Jin et al., 2019; Liu et al., 2020; Shu et al., 2020; Wang et al., 2020). To improve electrochemical performance of carbon materials, two main routes have been studied, namely optimization of the pore structure (Han, Cheng, Wang, & Wang, 2019; Han, Shi, Yan, Zhang, & Zhang, 2018) and introduction of pseudocapacitive reactions (Ania, Khomenko, Raymundo-Pinero, Parra, & Beguin, 2007; Xu, Allah et al., 2019). However, each strategy has certain drawbacks. Commonly, increases of SSA and pore volume cause the energy densities of electrodes to decrease. Moreover, pseudocapacitive active modifying materials, eg. metal oxides and conductive polymers, decrease the specific surface areas and the overall conductivity of the electrode materials (Hu et al., 2018; Pang, Wei, Wang, & Xia, 2018). All these issues have motivated investigations into syntheses of carbon electrodes through more efficient methods.

Carbon quantum dots (CDs), a zero-dimensional carbon material with sizes smaller than 10 nm, have been popular owing to their pronounced quantum confinement and edge effects (Wu et al., 2018). CDs have been used to control morphology and as conductive agents for fabricate composites. Such materials have achieved improved electrochemical performance in applications including fuel cells, supercapacitors, and lithium-ion batteries (Li, Rui, Song, Shen, & Zeng, 2015; Semeniuk et al., 2019; Xia et al., 2019). Current researches indicate that CDs doped composites have great potential as superior electrode materials. For example, Xiong and coworkers (Pang et al., 2018) reported that CDs can be used as guests on electrode surfaces to absorb more cations and achieved high specific energy densities on calcination of carbon dots-hydrogel composites. Ji and coworkers prepared MnO2/CDs/GO composite electrodes and suggested that the CDs acted as a bridge to connect MnO2 and graphene oxide (GO) (Lv et al., 2018). Also, Zhu et al. used CDs as an intercalator to prepare composite aerogels with excellent electrochemical performance and suggested that CDs could be used as a conductive agent (Zhu et al., 2015). These studies have shown that CDs have great promise for applications in electrochemistry.

In the design of CDs electrodes materials, graphene oxide (GO) is an optimal material for forming composites with CDs (Xu, Wang, Gao, & Jin, 2019; Zhang, Jiang et al., 2017; Zhang, Wang et al., 2017). Graphene-based composites typically have large surface areas, high electric conductivity, high porosity, and excellent mechanical properties. These features make such materials promising candidates for lightweight, compressible, and interconnected aerogels, needed for applications in flexible electronic devices (Li, Liu, Sun, & Gao, 2015). However, graphene nanosheets tend to aggregate, which lowers their accessible surface area and disrupts pathways for diffusion of ions (Cong, Chen, & Yu, 2014). To avoid re-stacking of graphene nanosheets, cellulose nanofibrils can be used as nanosupports to reduce deformation or shrinkage of GO aerogels (Zaman et al., 2020). And the composite of cellulose and GO has become a popular trend in the energy storage (Li et al., 2020; Zhang, Jiang et al., 2017; Zhang, Wang et al., 2017; Zou et al., 2019).

In the present work, we propose that CDs can improve the electrochemical performance of composite carbon aerogels. A compressible anisotropic lamellar ternary carbon aerogel of reduced graphene oxide (rGO)/carbon dots (CDs)/carbon microfiber (CMF) was synthesized via bidirectional freeze-casting, freeze-drying, and carbonization of GO, CDs and microfibrillated cellulose. The large planes of graphene provide more pathways for easy access and diffusion of ions when applied in supercapacitors and deionization. Furthermore, the microfibrillated celluloses prevented re-stacking of graphene sheets leading to the formation of a three-dimensional macroscopic assembly. The as-formed three-dimensional framework is an ideal scaffold for functionalization by guest N, P, and O from the co-doped CDs. The CDs scattered in the carbon aerogel provided the desired N, P, and O contents, and an abundance of electrons to form electron-rich regions capable of absorbing more cations. These features improved the electrochemical performance without sacrificing density, stability, or specific surface area. The CDs also regulated the type of nitrogen-containing functional groups formed in the composite. The addition of CDs reduced the formation of quaternary nitrogen by hydrothermal carbonization and thus increased the electrical conductivity of the composites. The added CDs created interfaces between GO/CD, CD/CMF, and GO/CMF, which thus reduce the charge transfer resistance. Consequently, the GO/CDs/CMF composite has ultralow resistance, a high SSA, and excellent electrochemical properties. These features improve the capacitive performances of the rGO/CDs/CMF composites.

Raw kapok fibers were purchased from Nanning, China and used as the precursors for CA. Sodium chlorite, acetic acid, absolute ethyl alcohol, adenosine triphosphate (ATP), folic acid and N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF) were purchased from Shanghai Aladdin Chemical Reagent Co., China. Other reagents were all of analytical grade. All chemicals were used as received without further purification. Detailed informations about characterization are shown in supporting information.

A 0.1-g portion of ATP and 0.05 g of folic acid were added to 15 mL of DMF in a 30 mL of polytetrafluoroethylene autoclave.And it was heated at 140, 180 or 220 °C for 10 h, and cooled. The obtained suspension was centrifuged at 10000 rpm for 10 min. The supernatant was added to a mixture of petroleum ether and ethyl acetate to obtain the solid. The obtained samples are denoted CDx, where x = 140, 180, and 220.

First, lignin was removed from kapok fibers by a treatment with acidified NaClO2 solution at 75 °C until the kapok fibers became white. The obtained sample was named as Ho-fibers. Next, hemicellulose was removed with 4 wt% sodium hydroxide at 90 °C for 4 h. After rinsing with distilled water for 3 times, the obtained sample was named as Al-fibers. Al-fibers were formulated into a 0.8% suspension, and then put in an ultrasonic generator. The ultrasonication was kept for 15 min at 1200 W to isolate the nanofibers and obtain microfibrillated cellulose. The details about composition, surface charge and dimensions of microfibrillated cellulose can be found in Supprting Information.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of ternary carbon aerogel of rGO/CDs/CMF
ce:section.ce:para: The cellulose was mixed with CDs and GO at the mass ratio of 3:1:1. The mixture was stirred for 6 h and ultrasonicated for 20 min to form a homogeneous suspension, which was frozen by bidirectional freeze-casting and finally freeze-dried. And then, the aerogel was carbonizated in a furnace under N2 flow. The tube furnace was heated to 300 °C at a rate of 2 °C/min and kept 2 h. Subsequently, the temperature was increased from 300 to 800 °C at a rate of 5 °C/min and kept for 2 h. The obtained samples are denoted as rGO-CDx-CMF, where x = 140, 180, and 220.

As illustrated in Fig. 1 a, rGO-CD180-CMF was prepared by mixing, bidirectional freeze-casting, freeze-drying, and carbonization. Briefly, CDs with an average diameter of 3.87 nm (Fig. S3) were synthesized through a solvothermal method with the use of ATP and folic acid as raw materials. Note that these CDs are rich in functional groups, which can affect the electron distribution of the whole carbon aerogel (Pang et al., 2018). The CDs uniformly decorating on both sides of the rGO-CD180-CMF sheets (Fig. 1b) were introduced into the carbon aerogel by bidirectional freeze-casting, and improved the conductivity greatly. The conductivity paralleled to the lamellar structure of rGO-CMF, rGO-CD140-CMF, rGO-CD180-CMF and rGO-CD220-CMF are 7.3, 10.6, 14.2 and 12.4 S cm-1 respectively. HRTEM images (Fig. 1b inset) showed a lattice spacing of 0.21 nm, which corresponds to the (100) planes of the CDs (Kutahya et al., 2019). Specifically, the GO/CDs/CMF suspension was poured into a plastic box and tightly attached to an L-shaped copper plate, which was subsequently placed under liquid nitrogen. A temperature gradient formed in both the horizontal and vertical directions of the copper plate and the mixture. When ice crystals grow along one direction, the suspension is also subjected to a force in the vertical direction. Consequently, ice crystals form parallel columns in the vertical direction and grow along the horizontal direction to create parallel ice lamellae. (Chen et al., 2020) Upon bidirectional growth of ice during freezing, GO sheets, CMF and CDs are rejected by the ice crystals to form a unique bridged lamellar structure. This structure was confirmed from the regular and continuous micrometer-sized parallel lamellae morphology of rGO-CD180-CMF (Fig. 1c–e). In contrast, rGO-CD180-CMF that was not subjected to bidirectional freeze-casting had a disordered interconnected network structure (Fig. S4a, b). In comparing the results in Figs. 1c–e and S4b, c, we conclude that the addition of CDs does not influence the parallel lamellae but does promote an even distribution of N and P in the carbon aerogel (Fig. 1f-g). Notably, the bidirectional growth of ice crystals formed a unique bridge structure between the parallel layers (Figs. 1c–e and S4c, d).

In contrast to the brittle features of common biomass-derived CA (Alatalo et al., 2016; E, Li, Ma, Xu, & Liu, 2018; Sun, E et al., 2019; Sun, Li et al., 2019; Wan et al., 2019), the carbon aerogel in this report is capable of withstanding considerable deformation without collapse (Fig. 2 a and Video S1). Interconnected bridges act as springs, which hold each parallel layer, making the aerogels lighter and more compressible. The R/R0 of the carbon aerogel maintained very stable in the 20 cycles of the cyclic compression test at 50% strain, implying long working life and reliability (Fig. 2b). Additionally, carbon aerogel is a promising elastomer conductor due to its outstanding compression properties and high electrical conductivity. A LED was lit when connected in series with the carbon aerogel (Fig. 2c–e, Video S2). This result is attributed to the many contact points in the parallel layer.

The large lamellar structure provides an environment for rapid flow of electrolyte, whereas the surface functional groups contribute to the improved electrochemical performance. As shown in the FT-IR results in Fig. S7, all the rGO-CDx-CMF samples had many surface groups, including P-O and C-N derived from CDs. These findings are consistent with the x-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) results, which show that the main peaks located at the binding energies of 133.4, 284.8, 401.0 and 532.2 eV in XPS spectra of rGO-CDx-CMF (Fig. 3 a) are ascribed to P 2p, C 1s, N 1s and O 1s, respectively. The above results confirm the successful dope of N and P atoms.

The N 1s spectrum (Figs. 3b and S9) was fitted into four different components at 398.2, 399.6, 400.9, and 403.4 eV, assigned to pyridinic N (N-6), pyrrole N (N-5), quaternary N (N-Q), and oxide N (N-X), respectively (Sun, E et al., 2019). We observed that the combination of CDs increased the proportion of N-Q. This phenomenon can be explained according to Pels, who claimed that unstable functionalities of nitrogen are converted to N-5 and then to N-6 under mild pyrolysis conditions (Pels, Kapteijn, Moulijn, Zhu, & Thomas, 1995). However, under harsh pyrolysis conditions, N-atoms are incorporated into the graphene layers by replacing C-atoms and eventually locate at edges as N-6 or the interior as N-Q. In this work, the N and P-atoms were pre-doped into the CDs by a solvothermal method. The solvothermal process provides sufficient time to convert unstable functionalities, such as amines and ammonium into N-5 and N-6. Then N-5 and N-6 attached to CDs are added into the aerogel during carbonization. In this process, N-5 or N-6, rather than unstable functionalities, are incorporated in the graphene layers directly through condensation of the carbon matrix replacing carbon atoms to form N-Q. This method can speed up the formation process of N-Q, and increase the proportion of these groups in the same carbonization time.

In the P 2p spectrum (Figs. 3c and S9), peaks at 134.1, 133.3, and 132.7 eV, are assigned to P-O, P-N, and P-C, respectively (Kim et al., 2019). The P-O feature is fitted to P 2p peaks of rGO-CD180-CMF because CDs formed by hydrothermal methods are rich in oxygen-containing functional groups. Moreover, the presence of P-O and P-N contributes to the formation of electron-rich regions. The functional groups introduced by the CDs increase defects and alter the charge distribution of the composite (Fig. 3d). The N, P, and O were introduced into the carbon skeleton and also changed the original structure of the carbon atoms. Therefore, functional groups and their neighboring regions act as centers that attract cations and accelerate charge transfer, which in turn improves the electrochemical performance of these carbon materials (Pang et al., 2018).

In addition, the disappearance of the sharp peak at 10.9° from GO and the appearance of a broad peak at ∼26°, indexed to the graphitic stacking of (002) in XRD patterns (Fig. 3e) (Singh et al., 2019), confirmed the formation of graphitized skeletons in rGO-CD180-CMF after reduction of GO. Further, the Raman spectra (Fig. S11) clearly display the well-defined broad disorder-induced D-band peak at ∼1350 cm -1 and in-plane vibration G-band at ∼ 1580 cm -1. The ID/IG is 0.96, 1.00, 1.04 and 0.98 for rGO-CD0-CMF, rGO-CD140-CMF, rGO-CD180-CMF and rGO-CD220-CMF. Moreover, the ID/IG values of rGO-CD140-CMF, rGO-CD180-CMF and rGO-CD220-CMF increase to different extent, implying that N and P are introduced and enhance the disorder degree of carbon matrix. As shown in Fig. 3f, the isotherm exhibited typical type IV curves, which rapidly increased in the low-pressure range (p/p0 < 0.02) and an obvious hysteresis loop characterizing the existence of both micro- and mesopores (Sun, Li et al., 2019). Moreover, abundant mesopores (Fig. 1b) and worm-like micropores (Fig. S12) were observed in the TEM images. These results suggest that the rGO-CD180-CMF has a 3D hierarchical porous structure with interconnected micro-, meso, and macropores. Notably, introducing heteroatoms into a composite material through the use of CDs as a carrier did not reduce the SSA (as shown in Table 1 ). Conversely, the SSA (601 m2 g-1) of rGO-CD180-CMF slightly increased than that (559 m2 g-1) of rGO-CMF owing to the abundance of rGO/CD, CD/CMF, and rGO/CMF interfaces introduced by CDs.

On the basis of the excellent wettability, electron-rich regions, high degree of graphitization and well-developed hierarchical porous structure, we expect the rGO-CDx-CMF composite can be used as elctrodes of high-performance supercapacitors. Fig. 4 a, b shows the CV and GCD curves of rGO-CDx-CMF and highlights the change of the electrochemical performance based on the species of CDs. The CV curves of rGO-CD140/180/220-CMF were quasi-rectangular shape with a few humps indicating coexistence of electric double layer behavior and psedocapacitance. The enclosed area of the CV profile and discharge time are qualitative indicators of capacitance. The calculated specific capacitances from GCD that rGO-CD140-CMF, rGO-CD180-CMF, rGO-CD220-CMF, and rGO-CMF were 192, 330, 249, and 152 F g−1 respectively. The addition of CDs clearly increased the specific capacitance.

rGO-CD180-CMF had the largest area in CV curves and the longest discharge time, and thus, the highest capacitance, among all samples. This difference is determined by the content and composition of N and P in the CDs. Fluorescence emission spectra and UV-Vis absorption in Fig. S5 together with the XPS results Table S2 confirm these features. In general, an appropriate amount of heteroatoms will improve electrochemical performance whereas, excessive doping will increase the internal resistance (Sun, E et al., 2019; Sun, Li et al., 2019). However, electrochemical performance continued to improve as the N, P, and O contents were increased in this work. rGO-CD180-CMF has the most N, P and O doping content, which are 4.74, 5.37 and 18.96 wt%, respectively. The reason why the good electrochemical performance is still maintained is that the CD carrier increases the site reactivity without reducing SSA. The highest proportion of N-Q greatly increased the conductivity, which offset the reduced conductivity effects of the heteroatoms. The Rs+Rct values of rGO-CMF, rGO-CD140-CMF, rGO-CD180-CMF, and rGO-CD220-CMF were 2.44, 2.15, 0.59, and 1.34 Ω, respectively (Fig. 4c). The almost negligible IR drop (Fig. 4b) and the low resistance also confirmed the good conductivity of rGO-CDx-CMF (Ling et al., 2015).

The rGO-CD180-CMF sample had the highest rate capability. When the scan rate was increased from 5 to 100 mV s−1, the CV curves (Fig. 4d) remained quasi-rectangular. The unconspicuous redox peaks may be resulted in sweep rate. As the scanning speed increasing, the electrode material has no enough time to undergo a redox reaction, thereby the redox peak becomes no longer obvious. Furthermore, rGO-CD180-CMF also exhibits good cycle stability o of up to 101.4% capacitance retention at 10 A g−1 over 5000 charge-discharge cycles (Fig. 4f). The increase in specific capacitance can be attributed to the electrochemical activation of the heteroatom during the cycling process.

To further confirm the enhancement effect of the CDs on CA, we used a three-electrode system based on 1 M NaCl, 6 M KOH, and 1 M H2SO4 aqueous solutions as electrolytes. There were no obvious redox peaks in 1 M NaCl (Fig. 5 a) and 6 M KOH (Fig. 5e), indicating that ion absorption is the main mechanism of operation. Specifically, the CV curves in 1 M H2SO4 had clear symmetrical hump-like peaks, indicating typical Faradaic reactions (Fig. 5i). Compared with rGO-CMF, rGO-CD180-CMF had an enlarged CV integrated area in all electrolytes, suggesting that the CDs increased the specific capacitance, through both capacitive and pseudocapacitive reactions. The GCD curves within a potential window of −1.0–0 V were measured to further investigate the enhancement effect of the CDs on electrochemical performance. Similar to the CV loops, the GCD curves in 1 M H2SO4 had isosceles triangle shapes with the two equal sides and a slight arc. (Fig. 5j) In addition, the IR drop can be negligible at start of discharge in all electrolytes, indicating a low equivalent series resistance (ESR). The specific capacitances of rGO-CMF and rGO-CD180-CMF calculated according to the GCD curves and formula (1) are shown in Fig. 5d, h, l. For rGO-CD180-CMF, the capacitance increased by 33%, 39%, and 31% compared with that of rGO-CMF at a current density of 1 A g−1 in 1 M NaCl, 6 M KOH, and 1 M H2SO4 electrolytes, respectively. Moreover, the performance of rGO-CD180-CMF was comparable to those of previously reported supercapacitor electrodes based on heteroatom doped carbon without CDs, as shown in Table S3.

EIS evaluates charge transfer and electrolyte diffusion at the interface of electrode and electrolyte interface. For example, the results in an acid electrolyte (1 M H2SO4) showed a semicircle in the high frequency range, a 45 °Warburg region, and a linear pure capacitor region at low frequencies (Fig. 5k). The spectrum of rGO-CD180-CMF had a more vertical line than that of rGO-CMF, indicating better capacitive behavior (Winter & Brodd, 2004). The rGO-CD180-CMF also had a higher knee frequency of 251.19 Hz than that of the rGO-CD180-CMF (39.81 Hz), suggesting better rate performance (Weng et al., 2011). There is a 45 °Warburg region on the actual impedance axis (Z ') in the intermediate frequency region, whose projection length characterizes the ion penetration process. A shorter Warburg length of rGO-CD180-CMF (from 251.19 to 0.05 Hz) indicated a shorter ion-diffusion path (Shao et al., 2018). The intersection of the semicircle in the high frequency region and the real axis is the solution resistance (i.e., Rs, resistance from the electrolyte). The intercept of rGO-CD180-CMF (0.39 Ω) was slightly smaller than that of rGO-CMF (0.47 Ω), which indicates that rGO-CD180-CMF favors access of ions (Zhang, He, Gao, & Li, 2019). Moreover, the contact interface resistance (i.e., Rc, the resistance from electrode/electrolyte interface) of rGO-CD180-CMF (0.18 Ω) was far smaller than that of the rGO-CMF (0.78 Ω), indicating that counterions passed through the electrode/electrolyte interface into the matrix at a higher rate (Liu, Lu, Sun, Chua, & Pan, 2015; Yao et al., 2018). Therefore, the EIS results showed that the rGO-CD180-CMF electrode is more effective at ionic and electronic transport than is rGO-CMF. As expected, (1) The abundant N, P, and O contained in the CDs improved the wettability of composite, which benefited interactions between the electrode and electrolyte for rGO-CD180-CMF. (2) The CDs scattered in the carbon aerogel gave the desired N, P, and O contents and introduced an abundance of electrons. These elements formed electron-rich regions capable of absorbing more cations and thus promoted electron transport without sacrificing stability and SSA. (3) Furthermore, the regulatory effect of the CDs gave the rGO-CD180-CMF more graphitic nitrogen, which effectively decreased the Rct by increasing the electrical conductivity of the composites.

The rGO-CD180-CMF composite has an abundance of graphic nitrogen, a large SSA, good wettability, and high capacitance, which suggest potential for application as an electrode material for CDI. The desalination performances of rGO-CD180-CMF and rGO-CMF were evaluated with the use of a homemade CDI testing setup, as shown in Fig. 6 e. The conductivity meter monitors changes of solution concentration in situ. To stabilize the desalination system, no voltage was applied during the first 20 min of the peristaltic pump operation (Fig. 6a). At this stage, the conductivity of the system slightly increased, because the material attached to the electrode surface entered the NaCl solution. Once the voltage was applied, the conductivity decreased sharply indicating that Na+ and Cl- rapidly adsorbed to the surface of oppositely charged electrodes. Gradually, the conductivity decreased, and stabilized at 80 min, indicating the ionic adsorption balance.

The effect of applied voltage on the desalination capacity was studied. As indicated in Fig. 6b, as the applied voltage increased, desalination capacity increased because of strong Coulombic interactions at high voltage. The rGO-CD180-CMF had desalination capacities of 9.6, 15.8, and 20.9 mg g−1 at voltages of 0.8, 1.0, and 1.2 V, respectively. The corresponding maximum ion removal rates were, respectively, 0.21, 0.28, and 0.35 mg g−1 min−1. The same trend was found in the Ragone plots showing the variation of initial NaCl concentrations (Fig. 6c). The Ragone plots shifted to the upper and right as the initial concentration was increased. At applied voltages of 1.2 V, the SAC/SAR reached 20.7/0.35, 32.6/0.69, and 57.1/0.81 mg g−1 min−1 in 33, 504, and 1000 mg L−1, respectively. This desalination ability exceeds the values for CDI applications listed in Table S4.

When the total amount of dissolved solids was higher than 500 mg L−1, the water was not drinkable. Hence, we set the initial salt concentration to be 500 mg L−1 to compare the desalination ability of rGO-CD180-CMF and rGO-CMF. As shown in Fig. 6d, the SAC of rGO-CD180-CMF was better than that of rGO-CMF throughout the whole process. Moreover, a 31% increase in desalination was achieved with the use of CDs. We attribute this improvement to CDs increasing the wettability and electrical conductivity of the composite, reducing the internal resistance, and adding more active sites. Moreover, charge efficiency, an important index to measure the effect of desalination, can be calculated from Fig. S14. rGO-CD180-CMF has a good charge efficiency of 0.72, indicating a good charge utilization.

A compressible ternary composite carbon aerogel of rGO/CDs/CMF with an excellent capacitive performance and deionization capacity is synthesized. The unique bridged layered structure of the composite formed by bidirectional freeze-casting gives the composite outstanding compressibility (up to 80% strain). Specifically, the addition of CDs adjusts the surface group composition, increases wettability, stability, conductivity and SSA, and reduces charge transfer resistance in the composite carbon aerogel. Consequently, the rGO-CD180-CMF has a large deionization capacity of 32.59 mg g−1 (initial NaCl concentration of 504 mg L−1), a high specific capacitance of 330 F g−1 (current density of 1 A g−1) and exceptional cycling stability (101.4% of its initial capacity retention over 5000 cycle at 10 A g−1), achieving a 117% increase in specific capacitance and 31% improvement in deionization capacity compared with those features of rGO-CMF. The incorporation of CDs into the composite carbon aerogel provides a new method for manufacturing electrodes with high performance in supercapacitors and deionization.

Jiaming Sun: Conceptualization, Methodology, Software, Investigation, Visualization, Writing - original draft. Yushan Liu: Data curation, Writing - review & editing, Software, Investigation, Visualization, Formal analysis. Zhenwei Wu: Validation. Mingcong Xu: Validation, Software, Data curation. Lei E: Validation. Chuihui Ma: Validation, Formal analysis. Sha Luo: Validation, Formal analysis. Ju Huang: Validation. Wei Li: Writing - review & editing, Supervision. Shouxin Liu: Resources, Writing - review & editing, Supervision.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The present work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (31890773, 31971601) and the Natural Science Foundation of Heilongjiang Province (YQ2020C016).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.carbpol.2020.117209.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following are Supplementary data to this article:
